coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots decorated CuS nanocomposite for effective degradation of methylene blue and antibacterial performance
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xingxian
Li, Luyao
Fu, Zheng
Cui, Fengling
coredata.dc:description: The development of conventional semiconductor photocatalyst is hampered by narrow wavelength response and high recombination rate between the photo-generated electron and hole pairs. Semiconductor composite technology is an effective method to enhance the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation by separating the photon-generated carrier or changing the band gap of semiconductors effectively. Here, we report a simple hydrothermal treatment to synthesize carbon quantum dots@cupric sulphide (CQDs@CuS) nanocomposite. The nanocomposite displayed a high efficiency to degrade methylene blue (MB) under visible light and antibacterial property to Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis). The findings could be explained that CQDs as electron donators could interact with CuS which resulted in the change of band-gap energy of CQDs@CuS nanocomposites. The lower band-gap energy increased their efficiency to photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes under visible light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Cupric sulphide
Photocatalytic degradation
Antibacterial performance

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study is financed by the National Natural Science Foundation of China -Union Foundation of Henan ( U1704170 ), Key Programs of Henan for Science and Technology Development ( 182102310103 , 182102310848 ), the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Henan Province ( 18IRTSTHN003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD pattern of CQDs, CuS and CQDs@CuS respectively, (B) FTIR spectra of the pure CQDs and CQDs@CuS nanocomposite.
(A) Schematic diagram for the fabrication, (B) SEM image, (C) EDX spectra of the CQDs@CuS nanocomposite.
(A) The TEM image of CQDs (inset: HRTEM image of CQDs), (B) the size distribution histogram of CQDs, (C–E) TEM images, (F) corresponding SAED pattern, (G) HRTEM image of the CQDs@CuS nanocomposite.
(A) XPS survey, (B) C 1s, (C) Cu 2p, and (D) S 2p spectrum of the CQDs@CuS nanocomposite.
(A) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm, (B) pore size distribution, (C) Raman spectrum, and (D) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQDs@CuS nanocomposite.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of MB solution with CQDs@CuS and H2O2 at different irradiation time, (B) the degradation rate of MB at specific time under various conditions (without catalyst, CQDs@CuS and H2O2 alone, CQDs@CuS and H2O2).
(A) Degradation efficiency in different pH, (B) the effect of H2O2 concentration on degradation efficiency.
The degradation rate for each run with CQDs@CuS nanocomposite.

(A) The polarization curves of the CQDs@CuS, (B), effects of benzoquinone (BQ, scavenger for superoxide radicals), tert-butyl alcohol (TBA, scavenger for hydroxyl radicals), disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA, scavenger for hole), AgNO3 (scavenger for electrons) on the photocatalytic activity of the CQDs@CuS.
Schematic presentation of dye adsorption, photogeneration of electron-hole pairs, and MB photo-degradation process on CQDs@CuS surface.
The bacteriostasis effect of different concentrations of catalyst (A–D: 0.6 mg/mL, E–H: 1.5 mg/mL) at different time (6, 12, 18, 24 h, respectively).

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots decorated CuS nanocomposite for effective degradation of methylene blue and antibacterial performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Henan Province for Green Manufacturing of Fine Chemicals, Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions, Ministry of Education, National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan 453007, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Henan Province for Green Manufacturing of Fine Chemicals
Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions
National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry Education
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of conventional semiconductor photocatalyst is hampered by narrow wavelength response and high recombination rate between the photo-generated electron and hole pairs. Semiconductor composite technology is an effective method to enhance the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation by separating the photon-generated carrier or changing the band gap of semiconductors effectively. Here, we report a simple hydrothermal treatment to synthesize carbon quantum dots@cupric sulphide (CQDs@CuS) nanocomposite. The nanocomposite displayed a high efficiency to degrade methylene blue (MB) under visible light and antibacterial property to Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis). The findings could be explained that CQDs as electron donators could interact with CuS which resulted in the change of band-gap energy of CQDs@CuS nanocomposites. The lower band-gap energy increased their efficiency to photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes under visible light.

The degradation mechanism and efficiency to MB of synthesized CQDs@CuS nanocomposite.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: It is a big challenge of organic pollutants in dyeing industry in the world with the fast development of industrial technology in the past half century [1]. Therefore, many methods, such as coagulation/flocculation, activated carbon adsorption, ozonation, electrochemical oxidation, and bio-treatment, were widely employed to remove the pollutants in water [2]. However, these methods cannot remove pollutants thoroughly. In 1972, Fujishima and Honda found out that semiconductor materials were able to degrade organic dyes in waste water and showed a potential solution to environmental sustainability [3–5]. After that, various semiconductor materials as photocatalyst have been investigated by industrial and academic organizations [6]. Nowadays, the researchers recognize that generally a single semiconductor catalyst exhibit low photocatalytic efficiency [7], and they make great efforts to improve it. Such as Xu et al. reported graphene-based and its derivatives-based semiconductor composites show an amazing effect in photocatalysis [7,8]. Similarly, there are many other composites which exhibit excellent photocatalytic performance, such as Au/CeO2 [9], meso-CuFe2O4 [10], CDs/BiVO4 QDs [11]. Thus it will contribute to the development of photocatalysis.

Copper sulfide (CuS), a p-type semiconductor material, has been used as a photocatalyst because of its excellent optical, electrical and catalytic properties [12]. Therefore, numerous methods including microwave-assisted method [13], hydrothermal method, solvothermal route, microemulsion, and wet chemical method et al. [14–16], have been developed to make nanostructured CuS to improve photocatalytic efficiency by changing morphologies or shapes, such as nanowires, nanoflakes, nanospheres [17–19], and flower-like structures et al. [15].

Recently, the CuS based composites have been reported which can enhance photocatalytic efficiency because the composites are able to separate the photon-generated carrier effectively or change the band gap of the semiconductors. Such as CdS/CuS [20], CuS/rGO [21] and CuS/ZnS [22] have been confirmed that they can enhance photocatalytic activity comparing to the pure CuS. In the last decade, CQDs has been one of burgeoning carbon nanomaterials (graphite-like particles) with monodispersion and small size, usually less than 10 nm in diameter [23,24]. With unique electron reservoir, photoinduced electron transfer property and stability, CQDs have been widely applied in photocatalysis research [25,26]. Scientists have prepared various CQDs composites such as CQDs/Ag3PO4 [27], CQDs/Bi2WO6 [28], CQDs/CdS [29] to improve photocatalytic efficiency.

Herein, we synthesized the CQDs@CuS nanocomposite by using a hydrothermal method. The photocatalytic performance of the nanocomposite was improved by CQDs, nearly 99% MB could be degraded in 40 min. Meanwhile, the Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis) was inhibited by nanocomposite at low concentration.

Copper acetate monohydrate, thiourea, ammonium citrate, ethylenediamine, hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), benzoquinone (BQ), tert-butyl alcohol (TBA), ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA), silver nitrate (AgNO3), methylene blue (MB) and H2O2 (30%, w/w) were purchased from the Sinopharm Chem. Reagent Co. These reagents were of analytical grade and without further purification. Ultrapure water was employed in all aqueous solution preparation and washing.

The CQDs were prepared based on a previous report [30] with some modifications. Ammonium citrate (0.2 g) and ethylenediamine (0.25 mL) were dissolved in ultrapure water (20 mL). Then, the mixture was transferred into a Teon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The products were collected by removing the large particles through centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 20 min and then filtrated with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The CQDs were stored at 4 °C for further characterization and use.

The preparation of CQDs@CuS nanocomposite was described as follows: 0.01 mol copper acetate was dissolved in 30 mL CQDs solution with magnetic stirring for 30 min to form a blue solution. 0.01 mol thiourea was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water and added to above solution drop by drop to form microemulsion with stirring. Then the microemulsion was transferred into a Teon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 10 h. Afterwards, the mixture was cooled to room temperature naturally. The obtained products were washed several times with ultrapure water and ethanol respectively, then dried in an vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) of the samples was characterized by X-ray diffractometer (XRD-D8 Advance, Bruker) with a Cu Kα radiation at a scan rate of 2° min−1. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Thermo-nicolet Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the material was analyzed using Thermo-Fisher electron spectrometer (ESCALAB250Xi). The surface morphology and internal structure of photocatalytic was performed on Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM-SU8010, Hitachi) and High-resolution TEM (HRTEM-JEM 2100). The elemental compositions of the samples were analyzed using an energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDX) equipped on the SU8010 FESEM. Raman spectrum of the sample was recorded using Airix (STR 500) instrument. Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms were performed on nitrogen adsorption apparatus (Micromeritics ASAP 2020). UV–Vis absorption spectra of the samples were executed using UV/VIS/NIR spectrophotometer (Lambda950, Perkinelmer) with BaSO4 as the reflectance sample. The electrochemical measurement was performed on electrochemical workstation (AUTOLAB, PGSTAT302).

The photocatalytic activity of nanocomposite was evaluated by photodegrading MB in aqueous solution under 500 W Xe Lamp. 25 mg photocatalyst was added into 100 mL 20 mg/L MB solution to form a suspension, then adjusted pH value of suspension with HCl (0.1 M) or NaOH (0.2 M). Before exposure, the suspension was kept in dark for 30 min under stirring to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Whereafter, definite concentration of H2O2 (30%, w/w) was added into the suspension and exposed under visible-light. During illumination, 5 mL of suspension was pipetted out at the certain time and centrifuged immediately to remove the photocatalyst. The absorbance of MB at 664 nm was analyzed using the UV–vis spectrophotometer.

The antibacterial assays of the CQDs@CuS nanocomposite were evaluated by using Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis) via well-diffusion method. 0.2 mL bacterial suspension (106 colony forming units (CFU) per mL) were taken out and smeared on the solid agar surface evenly. Two pieces of paper with diameter of 0.6 cm were placed on the agar plate separately, one piece of paper was added 0.02 mL of sterile water as a blank, another was added 0.02 mL of CQDs@CuS suspension with a certain concentration. The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 12 h and the bacteriostasis of the sample was observed by the size of the inhibition zone.

The XRD patterns of pure CQDs, CuS and CQDs@CuS were provided in Fig. 1A. From the XRD pattern of the pure CQDs, a broad diffraction peak was observed around 2θ = 25° which related to the graphitic (002) peak. To the pure CuS and CQDs@CuS, the major diffraction peaks were found at 2θ = 27.7°, 29.4°, 31.9°, 32.8°, 46.2°, 47.9°, 52.7°, and 59.3°, which were well indexed to (101), (102), (103), (006), (007), (110), (108) and (116) crystal planes of covellite CuS (JCPDS No. 06-0464) [31], and revealing that the adding of CQDs didn't change the molding of CuS. The intense and sharp diffraction peaks in the XRD patterns manifested high crystallinity of CuS component. However, the XRD peaks of CQDs were not observed in CQDs@CuS, due to the weak diffraction intensity and less content of CQDs in the CQDs@CuS.

In the FTIR spectra (Fig. 1B), the black and red lines represented the FTIR spectra of CQDs@CuS nanocomposite and CQDs, respectively. In the FTIR spectra of CQDs, the broad peak centered around at 3100 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and –NH2 [32–34]. The typical peaks at 1570 and 1658 cm−1 were associated to the stretching vibrations of CN and CO, respectively [35]. The peak at 1345 and 1040 cm−1 were ascribed to the vibration of CO [35,36]. As shown in the FTIR spectra of CQDs@CuS, the typical peaks at 3100 cm−1, 570 and 1658 cm−1 were also observed, which belonged to the stretching vibrations of OH, –NH2, CN and CO, and attributed to the CQDs of CQDs@CuS. In addition, the peak at 602 cm−1 was the stretching band of CuS [37]. The results indicated that the CQDs@CuS nanocomposite had been synthesized.

The morphology and crystallinity of the samples were characterized by SEM and TEM, respectively. Fig. 2A was the schematic diagram for the fabrication of the CQDs@CuS nanocomposite. Fig. 2B showed that the CQDs@CuS nanocomposite was assembled by spherical object under different multiple SEM. The EDX pattern of nanocomposite (Fig. 2C) showed that the nanocomposite contained Cu, S, and C elementals and did not have any impurities, which indicated that the CQDs@CuS nanocomposite had been synthesized successfully.

The TEM image and the size distribution information (Fig. 3A and B) of CQDs indicated that the CQDs had good monodispersity and the size mainly ranged from 3.2 to 5.2 nm with an average size of 4 nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. 3A inset) provided the obvious lattice planes with the lattice spacing of 0.32 nm, which was in good agreement with that of graphite facets (002 plane) [19]. The TEM images of CQDs@CuS sample were shown in Fig. 3C and D. And Fig. 3E and G showed that the CQDs deposited as small and inlaid on the surface of the CuS particles, with a lattice spacing of 0.32 nm and 0.28 nm, respectively. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 3F) provided four sharp rings from (102), (103), (110), and (116) planes of CuS, indicating its polycrystalline nature. All diffraction spots orderly indicated the highly crystallinity of CuS phase.

Chemical composition and valence states of CQDs@CuS nanocomposite were characterized by XPS. As shown in Fig. 4A, the survey spectrum displayed that the main component elements of the nanocomposite were C, S and Cu. In Fig. 4B, C1s spectrum was fitted into three separate peaks which was located at 283.7, 285.3, and 288.0 eV, corresponding to CC, CO, and CO, respectively [38]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of the Cu 2p peaks centered at 932.4 and 952.4 eV in Fig. 4C, which were associated with Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively [22,39]. The S 2p at XPS spectra in Fig. 4D, was split into two peaks centered at 161.3 and 162.7 eV, corresponding to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 states [40]. These results showed that the CQDs were successfully embedded on the surface of CuS particles, which were fully consistent with that obtained from XRD phase and FTIR spectra analysis.

Porous structure and the specific surface area of the as-obtained CQDs@CuS nanocomposite were investigated by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm analysis as shown in Fig. 5A. The result showed the type IV isotherm with hysteresis loops, proving the existence of both mesopores and macropores in the nanocomposite [41]. The specific surface area of the nanocomposite was calculated to be 23.54 m2/g and a total pore volume of 0.16 cm3/g. The corresponding pore size distribution (Fig. 5B) showed a wide distribution from 20 to 120 nm, further confirming the existence of mesopores and macropores in the CQDs@CuS nanocomposite.

The Raman spectra of CQDs@CuS nanocomposite was shown in Fig. 5C. The two peaks at around 1325 and 1584 cm−1 were attributed to the D (sp3-hybridized) and G (sp2-hybridized) bands of the CQDs, respectively. The D band was related to the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite or glassycarbon [42,43]. The G band was associated with the vibration of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms [42,44]. The intensity ratios of the disordered D band to the crystalline G band (ID/IG) was 1.085, indicating that they had a structure similar to graphite. Furthermore, it was observed that the D and G bands were not obvious, due to the low content of CQDs of the nanocomposite, which was consistent with the results of XRD. The very strong Raman bands at 468 cm−1 were associated with the SS stretching vibration in CuS [45], and there was no additional impurity peaks were present for any copper substrate and also un-reacted precursor compound. In summary, it indicated that the CQDs@CuS nanocomposite had been synthesized successfully.

As we all know that UV–Vis absorption spectra provide information relating to the optical properties and band gap of semiconductor nanoparticles well. For this purpose, UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the as-prepared CQDs@CuS nanocomposite was investigated. As shown in Fig. 5D, CQDs@CuS nanocomposite showed a strong absorption peak centered at 552 nm, indicating that the nanocomposite could be a promising photocatalytic material for absorbing the visible light. In addition, the band-gap energy of the CQDs@CuS nanocomposite was carried out using the absorption spectrum of the sample, according to the Eq. (1):
(1)
αhν = A
hv − Eg
1 / 2
where α is the absorption coefficient, hν is the incident photon energy, A is a constant, and Eg stands for the band gap energy, respectively [46]. According to the formula, the band-gap energy of the CQDs@CuS was calculated to be 2.25 eV, which indicated that an easier trend for CQDs@CuS to generate the electron-hole pairs by absorbing wider wavelength of light. Simultaneously, the narrow band gap was contributed to the separation of electron and hole pairs and inhibition their regeneration.

Additionally, according to the previous literature, the potentials of valance band (EVB) and conduction band (ECB) of a semiconductor material could be calculated via the following empirical equations [47]:
(2)
E VB
= X −
E e
+ 0.5 Eg
(3)
E CB
=
E VB
– Eg
where EVB is the VB edge potential, X is the electronegativity of the corresponding semiconductor, and the X value for CuS is about 5.28 eV [47], Ee is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (about 4.5 eV), Eg is the band gap energy of the semiconductor. According to the value of Eg and the foregoing formula, the calculated ECB and EVB of CQDs@CuS nanocomposite were −0.34 eV and 1.91 eV, respectively.

Organic dye sewage has been a pervasive threat to the environment. In this article, photocatalytic performance of CQDs@CuS nanocomposite to MB under visible light was evaluated and the degradation efficiency was calculated with Eq. (4), where C
0 is the original concentration of MB, and C
t is the concentration of pollutant at time.
(4)
Degradation efficiency
DE
=
1 −
C t
/
C 0
× 100 %

UV–Vis absorption spectra of MB solution mixed with nanocomposites under visible light (Fig. 6A) showed that the concentration of MB decreases gradually with time and eventually becomes zero. As can be seen in Fig. 6A, initially the concentration of MB was reduced in the presence of CQDs@CuS nanocomposite even without illumination, it might be attributed to the adsorption of catalyst. In addition, the MB dye self-photosensitization effect had been explored by doing control experiments. As shown in Fig. 6B, a blank experiment without CQDs@CuS nanocomposite showed that no obvious degradation of MB could be observed, which demonstrated that the reaction was truly driven by a photocatalytic process instead of the self-photosensitization process of MB [48,49]. Also the catalytic effect of CQDs@CuS or H2O2 alone under visible light was displayed in Fig. 6B. In the absence of H2O2, once the adsorption–desorption equilibrium was reached, the concentration of MB remained almost unchanged even under light irradiation. With CQDs@CuS nanocomposite alone, only 10% of MB was degraded in 40 min under visible light irradiation. However, with the presence of photocatalyst and H2O2, about 56% MB was degraded only in 2 min and nearly 100% MB was degraded in 40 min illumination.

In order to investigate the impact of pH on degradation rate, a series of pH values (5, 7, 9, and 11) were measured. As shown in Fig. 7A, the degradation efficiency of MB increased gradually with the increase of pH. Under the acid conditions, the positive charge on the catalyst surface was not conducive to adsorption of cationic dye MB. Thus, the degradation efficiency would decrease in acidic pH [50,51]. However, under the initial alkaline conditions (pH = 9, 11), the degradation efficiency of MB was increased obviously. It might be contributed to the negative charge on the surface of catalyst under the alkaline condition, which facilitating the migration of holes to the surface of catalyst and producing the OH radical by reacting with the electron [52].

The effect of H2O2 concentration is illustrated in Fig. 7B. It was clear that the degradation rate was increased obviously with the concentration of H2O2 increasing from 48 mM to 72 mM. But the further addition of H2O2 (96 nM) had rarely effect on the degradation rate. At a high dosage of H2O2, there were two distinct factors had to be considered: H2O2 generates more radicals available (OH) to degradation; and further addition of H2O2 had small effect on the degradation rate, because OH could react with H2O2 molecules according to the Eq. (5).
(5)
˙ OH +
H 2
O 2
→
HO 2
∙ +
H 2
O

The stability and recyclable use of CQDs@CuS composite were investigated. A five cycle test was carried out under 500 W Xe lamp irradiation. As shown in Fig. 8 , the degradation rate of MB could still reached to 92%. Therefore, the catalysts we synthesized had high stability and better photocatalytic effect.

As we all know, there were different reactive species such as the hydroxyl radical (·OH) and superoxide radical ion (O2 −), which were reactive intermediates and were generated upon the reaction of electrons and holes with H2O2 or H2O in the photocatalysis process [53]. In order to obtain the major reactive species in photocatalytic process, a series of controlled experiments with adding different scavengers were performed. As displayed in Fig. 9A, when the ·OH and O2 – scavengers of benzoquinone (BQ) and tert-butyl alcohol (TBA) were added in the reaction system [49,50], the photocatalytic effect decreased slightly. However, when a hole (h+) scavenger of disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA) was added [54], the photoactivity of CQDs@CuS nanocomposite was inhibited, and the degradation efficiency reduced to 66.1%, indicating that holes had an influence on degradation of MB in the reaction process. When the electrons (e−) scavenger of AgNO3 was added into the reaction system [48], the photocatalytic effect reduced to 35.4%. Therefore, the electrons were the dominant species and actively took part in photocatalysis [53]. Furthermore, the photoelectrochemical (PEC) characterizations of the samples were researched to learn the origin of the enhanced photocatalytic performance over the CQDs@CuS nanocomposite. The polarization curves of the samples (Fig. 9B) showed that CQDs@CuS displayed a higher current density at a similar potential range than pure CuS, indicating that the CQDs acted as electrons sink, which promoted the charge carriers separation and hence enhanced the photocatalytic activity of CQDs@CuS nanocomposite [55,56]. The mechanism of photocatalytic process of the CQDs@CuS samples under visible light irradiation is exhibited in Fig. 10 . When the CQDs@CuS nanocomposite was irradiated by visible light, the electron-hole pairs would be generated from the CuS material. And the electron transferred to CQDs, which facilitated the separation of electron-hole pairs. The mechanism of the photodegradation of MB explained in the above process is shown in the following equations, which was somewhat identical to those reported in literature [57].
(6)
CQDs @ CuS + hν →
e −
CQDs
+
h +
CuS
(7)
h +
+
H 2
O 2
→
H +
+ ˙ OOH
(8)
e −
+
H 2
O 2
→ ˙ OH +
OH −
(9)
h +
+
H 2
O → ˙ OH +
H +
(10)
e −
+
O 2
→ ˙
O 2
−
(11)
h +
+ MB → final product
(12)
˙ OH + MB → final product

The antibacterial activity of CQDs@CuS samples were performed by well-diffusion method against B. subtilis, and time-online data for antibacterial test were exhibited in Fig. 11 , the bacteriostasis effect were recorded every 6 h, and it was clear that the inhibition zone was increased with the increase of time and the concentration of samples due to proper diffusion of particles in medium with strong interaction with bacterial cell surface [58]. It indicated that the photocatalyst we synthesized had superior bacteriostasis to B. subtilis. This might be explained by the following reasons: firstly, the CQDs@CuS nanocomposite had strong tendency to accumulate the surface of bacteria cell and interaction with bacterial cell surface, thereby enhancing the permeability of cell membrane [58,59]. Secondly, it was due to the release of Cu2+ of CQDs@CuS nanocomposite, which could damage the bacterial cell membrane and enter cells to disrupt enzyme function, for example, forming copper–peptide complexes to catalyze the formation of reactive oxygen species (OH, O2 −, H2O2) and ultimately lead to cell death [59,60].

In this paper, the CQDs@CuS nanocomposite was synthesized successfully by a simple hydrothermal treatment. The CQDs were effectively attached to the surface of CuS material. The photocatalytic activity of CQDs@CuS nanocomposite was improved significantly compared with that of pure CuS materials. About 99% MB degradation was achieved during 40 min. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of CQDs@CuS nanocomposite was ascribed to the efficient charge separation and the inhibition of the recombination of electron-hole pairs owing to the electronic interaction between CQDs@CuS. The formation of large inhibition zone against pathogens B. subtilis via well-diffusion method indicated that small amount of photocatalyst powder had strong tendency to inactivate the pathogens. Thus, the CQDs@CuS photocatalyst had the potential in application for degrade the organic pollutants rapidly and inhibit the growth of bacteria effectively.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study is financed by the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Union Foundation of Henan (U1704170), Key Programs of Henan for Science and Technology Development (182102310103, 182102310848), the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Henan Province (18IRTSTHN003).
